## [03:03 - 06:04]

us. And so here when I went to start my business and I didn't know how to sell, I didn't know how to market. I went back in time and I learned there was this body of work about direct marketing. And that body of work went back way into like the 1920s and that was mostly based around direct mail where you would send out something via direct mail. And what I learned was there was three keys in direct marketing. And it was list, which was the list that you were reaching out to that you were mailing to. And then there was also the offer, which is what you were offering people. What you wanted them to buy. And then there was copy and copy or copy writing. That was the words you used in your direct mail or in your ad to get people to buy. And so what I learned is if you got two of these right, like list and offer or the list and the copy, you could do well. And if you got all three of these right, then you had a home run. I also learned that getting these three right was really complicated and really hard. And that's why there weren't that many people. There were people that had wild success in direct marketing, but there weren't very many of them. And there were a lot of people that tried it and failed. Well, what I also started to figure out then was that the online world over here was a little bit different. There were some key differences. One, it was more interactive. And two, it was a lot faster. You could get feedback a lot faster. Those two things made a huge difference. And also the third was that you weren't dependent on one piece of mail. The cost structure was different. Here you'd send out one piece of mail. It would cost you a lot of money. You would have to buy the list. Then you would have to write your copy. Then you would print it out and you would send it out. And then you sat around and waited for the response to come in. Here you could send multiple pieces of copy. So those three things really made a big difference. That it was more interactive, that the speed of response was quicker, and that the cost structure was different. You could afford to send multiple pieces of copy. And so somehow I inherently figured out that this was a little bit different. And then it allowed trial and error. So right here in 1986, I did my first launch and I did $1,650 in sales. Now everyone's frame of reference on money is different.
